bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the vote as an anti-war rebuke to Trump and a constitutional check, spotlighting phrases like “rebuke,” “defied,” and “end Iran war ,” while right-leaning coverage turns the same move into a story of betrayal and political calculation, stressing “reverses,” “primary loss,” and especially “RINO loser” or “joins Dems.” Cassidy’s switch becomes the key dividing line: the left reads it as a consequential defection, the right as opportunism after electoral defeat.
Both sides agree on the 50-47 advance and four GOP defections, but they diverge sharply on whether that signals principled restraint or partisan sabotage.
summary: On Tuesday, the Senate advanced a resolution to limit President Donald Trump's war powers in Iran, discharging it from committee in a 50 to 47 vote after seven failed attempts.
Louisiana Sen. Bill Cassidy switched sides to join Democrats, marking his first time supporting such a measure after Republicans had previously blocked similar Iran war powers resolutions.

Led by Democratic Sen. Tim Kaine of Virginia, the resolution gained support from Republicans Susan Collins, Lisa Murkowski, and Rand Paul; Kaine cited rising gas prices ahead of Memorial Day as evidence of economic toll.
President Trump announced Monday he would not follow through with 'scheduled' attacks on Iran, though he would likely veto the measure if both chambers approved it.

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer urged Republicans to 'break the cycle' on war powers, as Democrats contend the procedural advance carries significance and could influence the president's thinking on the war.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the deal as a corrupt, secretive escape hatch, using terms like “weaponization,” “quietly,” “bans,” and “slush fund” to stress abuse of power and Trump’s protection from accountability.
Right-Leaning coverage flips that script, spotlighting a “free pass,” “weaponization,” and “forever barred” language as evidence of relief from political targeting and IRS overreach.

The sharpest dividing line is whether the $1.8 billion fund and the end of audits are portrayed as a scandal or a vindication, even as both sides agree the settlement permanently shields Trump, his family, and businesses from future tax scrutiny.
summary: On Tuesday, the Justice Department issued a directive barring the Internal Revenue Service from pursuing tax claims against President Donald Trump , his family, and his companies, resolving his $10 billion lawsuit over leaked tax records.

Trump, his two sons, and the Trump Organization sued the IRS for at least $10 billion in January after a former contractor leaked their tax returns; the settlement provides a formal apology but no direct monetary payout.

As part of the agreement, the Justice Department established a $1.776 billion "Anti-Weaponization Fund" to compensate individuals claiming unfair targeting; Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche called it "a lawful process for victims of lawfare and weaponization to be heard."

Democratic lawmakers and ethics watchdogs criticized the fund as a "slush fund," while Senate Majority Leader John Thune expressed discomfort; Blanche defended the initiative as providing accountability despite critics labeling the deal "corrupt."

Claims will be accepted until December 15, 2028, by the five-member commission overseeing the fund; Blanche would not rule out payouts for individuals convicted during the January 6, 2021, Capitol riot, fueling debate.
title: US government agrees to drop tax claims against Trump in broadening of IRS lawsuit settlement

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Trump’s Paxton endorsement as a disruptive, politically risky move, using loaded terms like “blow,” “waffling,” and “scandal-ridden” to stress Cornyn’s setback and Paxton’s baggage; some even widen the lens to possible Democratic gains.

Right-Leaning coverage, by contrast, casts the same moment as a pro-Trump, pro-GOP power play, with phrases like “Complete and Total Endorsement,” “MAGA Warrior,” “loyal,” and “showdown” turning Paxton into a valued ally and Cornyn into the weaker, insufficiently aligned figure.
Both sides share the basic race mechanics and runoff timing, but diverge on whether this is party damage or ideological reinforcement.
summary: President Donald Trump issued a highly anticipated endorsement of Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton on Tuesday, dealing a substantial political blow to incumbent Senator John Cornyn just one week before their primary runoff election.
In a social media announcement, Trump hailed Paxton as a "true MAGA Warrior" while rebuking Cornyn for being slow to support his previous presidential campaign when times were difficult.

The endorsement directly follows months of intense lobbying from both campaigns and concludes a fierce, $25 million advertising battle that saw the candidates trade highly personal attacks on the airwaves.
The high-stakes May 26 runoff election comes after Cornyn narrowly edged past Paxton by just one percentage point in the initial multi-candidate March primary.
title: Trump endorses Ken Paxton over Sen. John Cornyn ahead of Texas Republican Senate runoff

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Minnesota ’s move as a more sweeping regulatory crackdown, stressing “outlaw,” the felony penalty, and the public-safety bill SF4760, while right-leaning coverage reduces the story to a straightforward legal fight, spotlighting the Trump administration “sues” line and de-emphasizing the harsher implications.

The sharper divide is between left-leaning concern over government action against companies like Kalshi and Polymarket and right-leaning skepticism of the ban itself, which is portrayed as restrictive.
Center coverage sits between them, widening the lens to a broader state-vs-federal standoff over regulation.
All sides agree on the first-in-the-nation ban and federal lawsuit, but differ on whether it signals protection or overreach.
summary: Minnesota became the first state to ban online prediction markets like Kalshi and Polymarket, making it a felony to operate, advertise, or assist access to such platforms starting August 1.
The law also bans tools such as VPNs that help users evade the ban and includes an expected exception for weather-related contracts used by farmers to hedge crop risks.
The Commodity Futures Trading Commission sued to block Minnesota's law, claiming exclusive federal jurisdiction over prediction markets and stating that the state's move is illegal and harmful.
Similar legislation has been introduced in several other states, with ongoing conflicts between federal and state authorities over regulation of prediction markets.
title: Minnesota becomes first state to ban prediction markets

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as a labor settlement and commuter relief, leaning on phrases like “fair deal” and “historic strike” while stressing phased reopening, rider headaches, and even alternative travel options.

Right-Leaning coverage shifts the emphasis toward public disruption and order, using sharper language such as “chaos” and “disruptive walkout” to portray the stoppage as a burden on commuters; it also foregrounds the restoration of service and the political role of Hochul and the MTA.
A key dividing line is whether the deal is treated as a labor win or a disruption ended, though both sides agree on the strike’s scale, the 3,500 workers involved, and the return of service.
summary: The Long Island Rail Road strike will end after the Metropolitan Transportation Authority and unions reached a tentative agreement announced by New York Governor Kathy Hochul.
Unions representing about 3,500 workers initiated the strike due to disputes over pay and work rules with rail management.
Negotiations restarted on Sunday and Monday after Governor Hochul urged both parties to return to talks.
Service on the Long Island Rail Road will resume Tuesday at noon as part of a phased return, according to Governor Hochul.
title: Long Island Rail Road strike to end as MTA, unions reach tentative deal

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the race as Trump’s power play, stressing a “major victory,” Massie as a “nemesis,” and even an AIPAC angle, while right-leaning coverage turns Gallrein into a heroic winner with phrases like “GOODBYE,” “ousted,” and “A true American Patriot.” The key dividing lines are Trump’s influence, Massie’s defiance, and the race’s huge cost: the right spotlights the “more than $32 million” spending as scale, the left treats the outcome as broader political revenge, and both emphasize Trump endorsement and Gallrein’s win.
The tones split between combative celebration and strategic, conflict-driven interpretation, revealing deeper ideological divides over loyalty, nationalism, and party discipline.
summary: Republican Rep. Thomas Massie lost Kentucky ’s GOP House primary to Trump-backed challenger Ed Gallrein after a costly and closely watched race.
The Kentucky primary became one of the most expensive House primaries in U.S. history, with heavy spending from outside groups and strong involvement from President Donald Trump.
Massie, known for occasionally breaking with Trump and GOP leadership on issues including foreign policy and spending bills, was defeated by Gallrein, a former Navy SEAL endorsed by Trump.
title: Trump-Backed Ed Gallrein Defeats Thomas Massie in Kentucky GOP Primary, Marking Major Victory for Trump

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the 200-jet deal as a broader win for “cooperation,” stressing aviation as a “key area” and linking the confirmation to Trump’s visit, while right-leaning coverage narrows in on the trade mechanics and adds historical weight, highlighting Boeing ’s first “major” Chinese deal in nearly a decade and prior market “shut out.” The center stays more transactional, foregrounding the trade-truce extension and its expiry.

The rhetorical split is subtle but telling: the left uses upbeat terms like “key area” and “major,” the right leans on “shut out” and “first,” and all three converge on the same core facts, revealing a shared economic frame beneath different geopolitical emphasis.
summary: On Wednesday, China's Commerce Ministry officially confirmed an agreement to purchase 200 Boeing aircraft, marking the nation's first major order since 2017 following a summit between President Donald Trump and President Xi Jinping.
Boeing CEO Kelly Orthberg met with Chinese Premier Li Qiang in Beijing on May 14, 2026, alongside the high-profile summit aimed at stabilizing bilateral ties between the two nations.
The White House reported China committed to purchasing at least $17 billion in agricultural products through 2028, while both nations seek reciprocal tariff cuts on $30 billion worth of goods each.
Trump claimed the deal could eventually reach 750 planes, with Boeing stating they "expect further commitments will follow after this initial tranche" to reopen the Chinese market.

Negotiations to extend the tariff truce set to expire in November remain ongoing; Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told Reuters the administration is "not in a rush" to finalize export control agreements with Beijing.
title: China Confirms It Will Buy 200 Boeing Jets After Trump-Xi Summit

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as a U.S. accountability failure, spotlighting civilian suffering, “horrors,” and phrases like “appalling lie” or “baseless fabrication,” while the right leans harder into blame and outrage with “fails to take responsibility” and “massacre.” The pivotal divide is how the missile-base detail is used: both sides mention the school’s location on an “active” base, but the left treats it as part of a skeptical U.S. explanation, while the right uses it to stress the investigation’s “complex” wartime context.
Both converge on congressional scrutiny and responsibility, yet they split over whether the core story is tragedy, justification, or evasion.
summary: On Tuesday, Admiral Brad Cooper, commander of U.S. Central Command , testified that the investigation into the school strike in Iran remains complex because the site is located on an active Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps cruise missile base.

The February 28 strike in Minab killed 155 people, including 73 boys and 47 girls; reports from Reuters, CNN, and the New York Times suggest a U.S. Tomahawk missile caused the tragedy, though the Pentagon has not accepted responsibility.

House Armed Services Committee Ranking Member Adam Smith pressed Cooper on the delay, stating, "It's really pretty clear what happened there. But 80 days on, we have not taken responsibility for that attack."

Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baghaei rejected Cooper's characterization as a "baseless fabrication," asserting that striking an educational institution constitutes a "grave violation of international humanitarian law" and a war crime.

Cooper alleged that Iran has intentionally struck civilian areas throughout the Middle East over a thousand times since February, killing at least 300 civilians, and promised to release investigation findings once the probe concludes.
title: US probe into strike on Iran girls' school near conclusion, US admiral says

bias: Left-Leaning coverage casts Bob Brooks as a sympathetic “blue-collar” firefighter with “bona fides,” pairing that with a “Trump-aligned” Republican and a “high-stakes” showdown to elevate working-class appeal and Democratic momentum; right-leaning coverage instead stresses “party divisions,” “union boss,” and “most narrowly divided swing” territory, turning the same win into a sign of labor power and intraparty strain.
The center stays procedural, noting he will “attempt to flip” the seat.

Both sides agree the district is a competitive battleground and Brooks will face Ryan Mackenzie, but they diverge over whether the race is a populist breakthrough or a warning about partisan and union influence.
summary: On Tuesday, firefighter union leader Bob Brooks won the Democratic primary for Pennsylvania 's 7th Congressional District, besting a crowded field to secure the party's nomination.

In November, Brooks will face incumbent Republican Rep. Ryan Mackenzie, who flipped the competitive 7th District in 2024 by around one percentage point in a seat President Donald Trump carried by around 8 points.

A Bethlehem firefighter for 20 years, Brooks leads the Pennsylvania Professional Fire Fighters Association and has secured endorsements from the International Association of Firefighters and Teamsters Local 773.
Targeting the status quo, Brooks called Washington a "dumpster fire" while advocating for term limits as the district has already drawn more than $11 million in advertising spending.
Democrats need to net three seats nationwide to regain control of the House, making this Pennsylvania race one of several critical contests that will determine the chamber's balance.
title: Bob Brooks wins Democratic nomination for U.S. House in Pennsylvania's 7th Congressional District

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Rubio’s Sweden trip through “European angst,” “Trump reliability,” and “grows,” turning troop cuts, Iran-war fallout, and rising energy prices into a story of instability and doubt.

Right-Leaning outlets shift the lens toward strategy: “increased defense spending,” “greater burden sharing,” and a proactive India visit with trade, defense, and energy talks, even adding Arctic and Quad angles; the left de-emphasizes those details.
The center stays itinerary-driven, simply tracing the travel plan.
Both sides agree the NATO meeting and India stop matter, but they diverge on whether the trip signals crisis or diplomatic leverage.
summary: On Friday, Secretary of State Marco Rubio will attend the NATO foreign ministers meeting in Helsingborg, Sweden, addressing European concerns over U.S. reliability and troop commitments amid alliance tensions.

President Donald Trump's surprise announcement early this month to withdraw 5,000 American troops from Europe blindsided NATO, creating security gaps despite earlier U.S. promises to coordinate military moves with allies.

Vice President JD Vance disputed claims of troop reductions in Poland, stating "What we did is that we delayed a troop deployment," while The Pentagon clarified plans to cancel deployments to Poland and Germany.

Chancellor Friedrich Merz criticized U.S. strategy in the Iran war, claiming the nation was being "humiliated," a remark that angered President Donald Trump and highlighted deepening alliance friction.

Following Sweden, Rubio will travel to India to meet counterparts from the Quad grouping of Indo-Pacific democracies, while also focusing on Arctic security interests and a strengthened High North posture.
title: Rubio to visit Sweden for NATO meeting, then India

bias: Left and center coverage frame Doug Jones’s nomination as a straightforward win, with the center adding scale through “77 percent” and “crowded primary race,” while the right turns the same result into a partisan showdown, stressing Jones’s “steep uphill climb,” Alabama as “solidly red,” and Tuberville as “Trump-backed.” That contrast shows the left de-emphasizing drama, the center widening the lens with vote share and race importance, and the right using charged cues like “rally” and “Trump-backed” to foreground ideological conflict.
Both sides agree on the AP call and the matchup, but they diverge sharply on whether the story is a routine primary result or a broader sign of red-state political realities.
summary: Former Sen. Doug Jones secured the Democratic nomination for Alabama governor on Tuesday, setting up a November general election rematch against Republican Sen. Tommy Tuberville.
Republican Sen. Tommy Tuberville won the Republican Primary to succeed Gov. Kay Ivey, whose term limit prevents her reelection in 2026 and opens a Senate seat.
Jones campaigned on his courtroom record, including the 1963 prosecution of two members of the Klux Klan for the Street Baptist Church bombing in Birmingham.
Alabama Primary elections can trigger runoff contests if no candidate receives a majority of votes; Final certified results are expected in the coming days.
This general election represents one of the state's most closely watched races, forcing voters to choose between Jones and Tuberville in their 2020 Senate rematch.
title: Doug Jones wins Democratic nomination for governor in Alabama

bias: The left frames the dispute through the Trump administration and a broader crackdown lens, stressing the ban’s reach into sports prediction markets , while the right strips it to a sharper regulation-versus-freedom clash, using phrases like “Wall Street regulator” and “first-in-nation ban” to suggest overreach.
The center adds the most legal texture, highlighting “outright ban,” “criminalize,” and the Aug. 1 deadline, plus Gov.
Tim Walz’s role.
The key dividing line is whether the story is about political power and restriction or regulatory pushback; all sides agree Minnesota enacted the ban and the CFTC sued to stop it.
summary: On Tuesday, the Commodity Futures Trading Commission sued to block Minnesota from enforcing a new law that criminalizes prediction markets , which Governor Tim Walz signed to prohibit operations starting August 1.

Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison defended the ban, stating prediction markets are 'designed to be addictive' and 'prey especially on young people and low-income folks,' adding they help the wealthy at the expense of others.

CFTC Chairman Michael Selig stated the law 'turns lawful operators and participants in prediction markets into felons overnight,' while Polymarket added the state's action 'runs counter to the federal government's established framework.'

Federal regulators have pursued similar interventions against other states, recently securing a court order blocking Arizona from pursuing a criminal case against New York-based Kalshi; Nevada maintains an in-effect ban, while Massachusetts' highest court is weighing an injunction.

Under President Donald Trump, the agency maintains prediction markets fall exclusively under federal authority to regulate 'swaps,' making this clash between state criminalization and federal oversight the primary legal dispute for the $22 billion industry.
title: US regulator sues to block Minnesota's first-in-nation ban on prediction markets

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Tuberville’s win as a procedural step into a likely general-election “rematch,” stressing he is the “clear favorite” in a “high-stakes” contest; right-leaning coverage turns it into a celebratory MAGA moment, using terms like “Trump ally,” “cruises,” “Secures,” and “WINS” to signal momentum and triumph.
The sharpest divide is Trump’s endorsement: left treats it as context, while right makes it the headline logic, alongside Alabama ’s “deep-red state” identity.
Both agree Tuberville won and Jones awaits, but they diverge on whether the story is electoral setup or ideological victory.
summary: On Tuesday, Republican Sen. Tommy Tuberville won Alabama 's Republican gubernatorial primary, securing the party's nomination with a commanding victory. He is now the presumptive favorite to become the state's next governor this fall.

Choosing to forgo a second Senate term, Tuberville entered the race as the presumptive frontrunner. He consolidated early support, positioning himself as the clear choice for the gubernatorial nomination.

Seven Republican candidates appeared on the ballot, including Rep. Barry Moore and Alabama Attorney General Steve Marshall. Tuberville defeated challengers like businessman Will Santivasci and Ken McFeeters, whose residency lawsuit was dismissed by a judge.

Heading into the general election on November 3, 2026, Tuberville is considered the heavy favorite. The campaign will now shift to the general-election phase as the party prepares for the upcoming November contest.

Several Democratic candidates are vying for the nomination to oppose Tuberville, including former Senator Doug Jones, who served in the Senate before his defeat. The field includes businessman Will Boyd and other contenders seeking to challenge the Republican nominee.
title: Sen. Tommy Tuberville wins Republican primary for governor of Alabama

bias: Left-Leaning coverage treats Alvarado mainly as a primary result, but when it invokes Trump it does so as a framing device—“Trump-backed candidate”—that hints at broader partisan stakes and the seat being “replace Barr.” Right-leaning coverage, by contrast, turns that same endorsement into the story, using celebratory language like “glowing,” “True friend,” and “pro-MAGA” to cast the win as a conservative triumph and “critical” moment.

The sharp dividing line is Trump’s role and ideological branding; both sides agree on the nomination outcome and Alvarado’s physician/state-senator profile, yet they reveal different priorities in America’s wider culture-war politics .
summary: Ralph Alvarado, a Kentucky state senator and former physician, won the Republican nomination for Kentucky's 6th Congressional District and will run in the general election.
President Donald Trump endorsed Alvarado, praising him as a consistent MAGA supporter who helped mobilize Hispanic voters in 2016.
Alvarado has positioned himself as a pro-Trump, America First candidate, emphasizing his consistent support for Trump in multiple election cycles.
The district has historically leaned heavily Republican, with incumbent Andy Barr winning reelection in 2024 by a 63% margin before pursuing a U.S. Senate seat.
title: Ralph Alvarado wins Republican nomination for U.S. House in Kentucky's 6th Congressional District

bias: Left coverage frames the runoff as a “bitter intraparty fight” that delays Republicans’ effort to target Jon Ossoff and could shape Senate control, while right-leaning coverage casts it as a “battle” to unseat a “vulnerable” Democratic incumbent and recalls the “once-red seat” to signal opportunity.

The right’s rhetoric is more combative and partisan, with words like “GOP,” “battle,” and “vulnerable,” while the left leans on strategic stakes and broader implications, using “critical battleground” and emphasizing delay.
A center voice briefly adds “MAGA workhorse” and candidate background.
Both sides agree on the runoff, but diverge over whether it signals Republican momentum or Republican complication.
summary: Rep. Mike Collins and Derek Dooley advanced to a June 16 runoff after no primary candidate reached the 50 percent threshold required to avoid one in Georgia's consequential Senate race.
Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp backed Dooley as an outsider while Collins emphasized his conservative record and support for President Trump, amid intense infighting that marked the primary contest.

The GOP-aligned Senate Leadership Fund pledged $44 million for the fall, while the Democratic-aligned Senate Majority PAC committed $20 million, positioning this as one of the nation's most expensive races.

Sen. Jon Ossoff leads in hypothetical general-election matchups, with campaign spokesperson Ellie Doughtery stating any GOP nominee will be "bruised and terminally inseparable from the toxic president."
This runoff intensifies pressure on Trump to intervene; Sen. Raphael Warnock, who won in 2023, warned that internal party battles can be detrimental to Republican success.
title: Georgia GOP Senate primary heads to runoff as Republicans battle to unseat Ossoff

bias: Left-Leaning coverage casts Rabb’s primary as a celebratory “win” for progressives, stressing “anti-establishment” energy and even describing the race as “heated,” while right-leaning coverage pivots sharply to ideology, foregrounding “AOC backing,” “anti-Israel,” and “the Squad” to frame him as controversial.
That divide turns on one pivotal fault line: whether Rabb is a progressive breakthrough or a left-wing liability.
Center coverage sits between them, supplying district, opponent, and projection details without the same praise or alarm.
Both sides agree he won an open Philadelphia seat, but they diverge on whether that result signals momentum or concern within broader cultural and geopolitical battles.
summary: On Tuesday, CBS News projected State Rep. Chris Rabb won the Democratic primary for Pennsylvania's 3rd Congressional District, poised to succeed retiring Rep. Dwight Evans.
The contest revealed Democratic fault lines, with Rabb describing himself as "an aggressively anti-establishment Democrat" while defeating state Sen. Sharif Street and physician Ala Stanford.
Backed by the "Squad" and the Working Families Party, Rabb campaigned on Medicare and the Green New Deal, urging rivals to label Israel's war in Gaza a "genocide."
Rabb's victory marks a rebuke of Philadelphia's Democratic machine, with him saying "that the era of establishment politics is coming to an end."
Given the 3rd Congressional District's heavy Democratic lean and the absence of a Republican challenger, Rabb is widely expected to win the seat in November.
title: Chris Rabb wins Democratic primary for House seat representing parts of Philadelphia, CBS News projects

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Bottoms’ win as a historic breakthrough, spotlighting “first Black woman governor,” “make history,” and even a “head start,” while also nodding to concerns about her Atlanta mayor record.

Right-Leaning coverage strips away the celebratory tone, leaning into horse-race skepticism with phrases like “losing streak,” “Ex-Biden official,” and “avoids runoff,” treating the result as a test of Democrats’ weakness rather than representation.
The sharpest divide is whether the race is a symbolic milestone or proof of party performance.
Both sides agree she won and faces a tough November fight in Georgia’s long Republican drought.
summary: On Tuesday, former Atlanta Mayor Keisha Lance Bottoms won the Democratic nomination for Georgia governor, setting up a November election for a seat her party has not won in 24 years.

Bottoms, the only Black woman in the Democratic field, leveraged a rare endorsement from President Joe Biden after serving in his administration, becoming the party's third consecutive Black woman nominee.
Republicans poured more than $125 million into advertising, far outpacing the $4 million spent by Democrats; Gov. Burt Jones and Rick Jackson advanced to a June runoff with Trump's backing of Jones.
Democrat Jasmine Clark won the nomination to succeed Rep. David Scott in the 13th Congressional District, while U.S. Rep. Mike Collins advanced to the Senate runoff against rival Buddy Carter.

The Judicial Qualifications Commission alleged on Sunday that two judicial candidates violated conduct rules, a move State Democratic Party Chair Charlie Bailey condemned as a "cynical attempt" by the Georgia Republican establishment.
title: Keisha Lance Bottoms wins Democratic nomination in Georgia governor’s race

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the strikes as evidence of a more active U.S. role in Nigeria , stressing the shift from “advisory and training” to direct involvement and highlighting the army’s hard-edged language like “hunt down and kill terrorists anywhere.” Right-leaning coverage, by contrast, stays terse and security-first, foregrounding the “joint US strikes” and the kill count as a counterterrorism win, with little interest in broader implications.
Center-Leaning coverage bridges both, adding AFRICOM’s strategic messaging and phrases like “capabilities” and “epicenter of global” terrorism.
All sides agree on the 175 deaths and northeast location, but diverge on what the operation signifies.
summary: On Tuesday, the Nigerian Defence Headquarters and United States Africa Command announced joint air strikes killed 175 Islamic State fighters in Nigeria's northeast, destroying checkpoints, weapons caches, and financial networks.

These strikes followed the killing late last week of Abu Bilal al-Minuki, the jihadist group's global second-in-command, marking the first successful targeting of a senior militant in over a decade of insurgency in Nigeria.

Military operations also neutralized senior leaders Abd-al Wahhab, Abu Musa al-Mangawi, and Abu al-Muthanna al-Muhajir, who coordinated attacks, propaganda, and media production for the militant group.
The joint operation signals active U.S. involvement beyond previous advisory roles; President Bola Tinubu thanked President Donald Trump for his "leadership and unwavering support."

AFRICOM commander General Dagvin Anderson stated Africa has become the "epicenter of global terrorism," warning that U.S. security interests depend on maintaining regional partnerships amid growing intelligence gaps.
title: Nigeria says joint strikes with the US have killed 175 Islamic State group fighters
‘Melodi’ Moment in Rome: PM Modi Gifts Melody Toffees to Giorgia Meloni During His Italy Visit

summary: On Tuesday, incumbent Justices Sarah Warren and Charles Bethel won reelection to the Georgia Supreme Court, defeating Democratic-backed challengers Jen Jordan and Miracle Rankin.

The typically quiet judicial races attracted over $4 million in advertising and high-profile partisan involvement, with former President Barack Obama endorsing challengers and Republican Governor Brian Kemp backing incumbents.
Election returns showed Warren defeating Jordan 59% to 41%, while Bethel led Rankin 51% to 49%; Kemp's leadership PAC spent $500,000 defending the incumbents.
Conservative control of the Georgia Supreme Court remains intact, with eight of nine justices initially appointed by Republican governors, preserving the court's ideological balance this year.
Three additional GOP-appointed members face reelection in 2028, raising the possibility that Democratic-backed candidates could flip the court's ideological balance in future contests.
title: 2 GOP-backed justices win Georgia Supreme Court reelection
Trump to attend G7 summit in France, White House official says

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the delay as a negotiated “deal with business leaders,” stressing that the $30 wage is only postponed and still phases up to $25 in July, then $30 in 2030; right-leaning coverage turns the same move into a business rescue, with alarmed language like “Business Apocalypse,” “averted,” and “business tax revolt,” portraying the council’s action as a win over government overreach.
The key dividing lines are the delay itself, the wage timeline, and whether business pressure is compromise or crisis.
Both sides agree the council revised the plan, but they split sharply on whether the story is labor progress or economic threat.
summary: On Tuesday, the Los Angeles City Council approved an 18-month delay to a $30 minimum wage for hotel and airport workers, postponing the increase until after the 2028 Olympics.
Business leaders threatened to repeal the city's gross receipts tax, which officials claimed would strip $740 million from the general fund over the first year, forcing negotiations.

Mayor Karen Bass brokered the agreement, calling the proposed tax repeal "an existential threat to the city budget and the services it supports." Business groups agreed to withdraw the ballot measure.
Finalizing the schedule, the 11 to 4 vote raises wages to $25 in July and reaches $30 by January 2030, though a second approval is required next week.

Economists remain divided on wage impacts; the Employment Policies Institute found 96% of 166 surveyed economists oppose a $20 minimum wage. Activist Saru Jayaraman counters that workers cannot survive on less than cost-of-living wages.
title: L.A. City Council Delays $30 Minimum Wage for Hospitality Workers

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Colbert as a beloved satirist, praising him with terms like “truthiness,” “salutes,” and “shared cultural ritual,” and treating CBS’s cancellation as politically fraught, even suggestive of Trump appeasement.

Right-Leaning coverage flips the script, using sharper attacks such as “painfully unfunny,” “sucking money pit,” “partisan,” and “embarrassment” to portray him as a divisive activist whose exit is deserved.

The key dividing line is whether the finale signals cultural loss or overdue housecleaning, with the Trump angle, CBS/Paramount’s motives, and late-night’s future shared across both sides but interpreted through starkly different ideological lenses.
summary: On Thursday, May 21, 2026, The Late Show with Stephen Colbert will air its final episode, concluding an 11-year run on CBS.

Paramount executives described the cancellation as "purely" financial amid a challenging late-night market, though critics allege the decision stems from Colbert's frequent criticism of President Donald Trump.
Starting Friday, Comics Unleashed with Byron Allen will occupy the 11:35 p.m. Eastern time slot, offering comedy that generally avoids the political satire characteristic of Colbert's production.
Colbert's final week features guests including Bruce Springsteen and Steven Spielberg, while peers like John Oliver and David Letterman have publicly praised the host as the institution ends.
title: Stephen Colbert Prepares for Final Broadcast as CBS Retires ‘The Late Show’

summary: On Tuesday, Oregon voters rejected Measure 120 with more than 83% opposing the referendum, effectively blocking a Democratic-backed bill that raised the state gas tax from 40 to 46 cents and increased transportation fees.

Republican leaders gathered signatures to force the measure onto the ballot after the Democratic-controlled Legislature passed the tax increase last fall to address declining gas tax revenue from fuel-efficient vehicles.
Citing soaring gas prices from the U.S. and Israel's war in Iran, voters overwhelmingly opposed the measure; 79-year-old Sister Barbara Julian of Bend said taxes were "too high" and needed to be cut.

Transportation agencies now face funding gaps for road and bridge maintenance as state leaders declined to actively campaign for the measure's passage, leaving infrastructure costs to potentially increase.

Governor Tina Kotek is now drafting a new funding plan for the upcoming legislative session, while Republicans aim to leverage the results to challenge national Democratic messaging about affordability in midterm elections.
title: Oregon voters reject Democrats’ gas tax increase as Iran war causes prices to soar

bias: Left-Leaning coverage casts the dispute as a constitutional clash, emphasizing the federal government’s claim that the law is “blatantly unconstitutional” and spotlighting “protected areas” plus the mask and ID rules as civil-liberties flashpoints.

Right-Leaning coverage flips the frame, presenting the measure as the “Act Concerning Democracy and Government Accountability,” a pro-oversight response to ICE operations, and using sharper terms like “illegal attempt” and “mask ban” to stress federal overreach.
Center coverage stays procedural and stripped down.
Both sides agree on the lawsuit, but diverge over whether the law is accountability-minded protection or an unlawful intrusion.
summary: On Friday, the U.S. Department of Justice filed a lawsuit against Connecticut, Governor Ned Lamont, and Attorney General William Tong over a state law prohibiting federal agents from wearing masks and requiring identification while on duty.

Passed this spring, Senate Bill 397 establishes "protected areas" including schools and hospitals where immigration arrests based on civil offenses are prohibited, while banning law enforcement officers from wearing masks.

Federal prosecutors argue the law violates the Supremacy Clause, warning that requiring agents to remove masks creates "hesitation, indecision, or second-guessing" during split-second use-of-force decisions.
Attorney General William Tong defended the measure on Monday as "fully lawful and necessary to protect public safety," while Democratic leaders dismissed the DOJ's challenge as "purely partisan."

Republican critics, including Senate Minority Leader Stephen Harding, called the law "unconstitutional," as the federal government pursues similar lawsuits against mask-restriction statutes in California, New York, and New Jersey.
title: US Justice Department sues Connecticut over law banning masks, requiring ID for ICE

summary: On Monday, South Dakota -based POET and Antora Energy launched a 5 gigawatt-hour thermal energy storage system adjacent to the POET ethanol plant in Big Stone City.

Antora Energy CEO Andrew Ponec selected Big Stone City for its position as a major hub on the MISO power grid, where concentrations of energy users exist. He likened the system to a giant toaster that stores wind energy as heat in carbon blocks.

Construction supported 300 jobs in South Dakota and California, while the system stores energy in carbon blocks reaching 4,000 degrees Fahrenheit to power POET's 92 million gallon annual ethanol facility.

Jeff Lautt, president and chief operating officer of POET, said the system captures excess wind energy that would otherwise be wasted, creating what he called a "win-win-win" that reduces natural gas usage.
title: One of World’s Largest Energy Storage Plants Launches in South Dakota

bias: Left-Leaning coverage stays tightly on Stelson’s primary win and “second straight challenge,” presenting the race as a straightforward nomination-to-general-election step, while right-leaning coverage widens the frame into a more strategic, partisan contest: it stresses a “key” or “tightly contested” district, calls Perry “one of the most endangered House Republicans,” and spotlights Stelson as a “news anchor-turned” candidate.

The tonal split is subtle but real—left is calm and procedural; right uses more horse-race urgency and labels like “Dem” and “challenge,” with issue references such as healthcare, costs, and reform serving to heighten stakes.
Both agree on the rematch, but differ on whether it is mainly a candidacy milestone or a vulnerable-seat showdown.
summary: On Tuesday, Janelle Stelson won the Democratic nomination to challenge Republican U.S. Rep. Scott Perry in the Harrisburg-area district, mounting her second straight bid against the incumbent.

Democratic Gov. Josh Shapiro and the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee backed Stelson to help retake the U.S. House, aiming to select "top tier" candidates, DCCC chair Rep. Suzan DelBene said.

Contested primaries include Bob Harvie in suburban Philadelphia challenging Republican U.S. Rep. Brian Fitzpatrick, and Bob Brooks in the Allentown-area seat challenging Republican U.S. Rep. Ryan Mackenzie.

Philadelphia Democrats face a wide-open primary to succeed retiring Democratic Rep. Dwight Evans, featuring state senator Sharif Street. Scranton Mayor Paige Cognetti secured the Democratic nomination unopposed.
Democrats hope to capitalize on weaker Republican turnout in Pennsylvania this year to flip key seats, as Shapiro aims to deliver Democratic control of the state Legislature.
title: Janelle Stelson wins Democratic primary in Pennsylvania to in 2nd challenge to US Rep. Scott Perry

bias: Left-Leaning coverage turns the Georgia runoff into a Trump-era drama, foregrounding “MAGA clash,” “Trump backers,” and “MAGA billionaire” to stress factional conflict and the broader stakes for a swing state, while the right strips the story down to a terse election update, simply saying Burt Jones “advances” and de-emphasizing Rick Jackson, the June 16 runoff, and wider implications.
The sharpest dividing line is ideology: left framing treats Trump alignment as the story, whereas center coverage stays with who advanced, the runoff structure, and the field’s top vote-getters.
Both sides, however, agree the contest is a Republican runoff in Georgia, showing shared emphasis on the race itself amid deeper partisan and cultural divides.
summary: Following Tuesday's primary, Gov. Burt Jones and Rick Jackson advanced to a June 16 Republican runoff after neither candidate secured more than 50% of the vote in Georgia's crowded gubernatorial race.

The primary race became extraordinarily expensive, with total advertising spending exceeding $113 million; Jackson fueled much of this, pumping $80 million of his own money into ads blanketing Georgia's airwaves.

President Donald Trump reiterated his official endorsement of Burt Jones, squashing speculation that Jackson's campaign could sway him, even as Jackson aggressively aligned himself with the president.
Jackson and Jones have fought each other in the courts, with Jackson filing a defamation lawsuit against Jones, turning the primary into a bitter struggle over campaign funding and resources.

Republicans have won every gubernatorial election in Georgia since 2002, and the eventual Republican nominee will face the Democratic winner in November, making this runoff critical for party control.
title: Jones, Jackson advance to runoff in Georgia GOP gubernatorial contest

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the ruling as a rights victory for “families” and “trans youth,” stressing “gender-affirming care,” “discriminated,” and even “despite threats from Trump,” while right-leaning coverage recasts the same order as a culture-war imposition, using skeptical phrases like “gender transitions,” “transgender procedures,” and “Blue State” to cast the court as partisan and the care as contentious for minors.
The key dividing lines are Trump-linked pressure, the age of the patients, and whether the hospital’s halt was capitulation or caution.
Both sides agree the 5-2 court order requires resumed care, but they split sharply on what that means ideologically.
summary: On Monday, the Colorado Supreme Court ordered Children's Hospital Colorado to resume gender-affirming care in a 5-2 decision, finding the facility likely violated state anti-discrimination law by suspending services.

The hospital suspended services earlier this year following warnings from U.S. Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. that providers offering such care could face exclusion from federal funding programs.

Justice William Hood III wrote that "denying access to puberty blockers and hormone therapy for gender-affirming care but not for purposes unrelated to gender-affirming care differentiates care based on gender identity."

The state's highest court directed the lower trial court to issue an injunction requiring the resumption of services, while a hospital spokeswoman said the facility is examining the ruling and assessing next steps.

Although Children's Hospital Colorado maintained that resuming care risks federal sanctions, the majority concluded that potential harm to patients outweighs the speculative threat of losing federal health care program funding.
title: Colorado’s top court orders children’s hospital to resume gender-affirming care for minors

bias: Left-Leaning coverage centers civilian harm and aftermath, using emotionally weighted terms like “children and women,” “bodies recovered,” and even Hezbollah’s “Israeli enemy” language to frame the strike as part of an ongoing humanitarian crisis that “escalat the conflict.” Right-leaning coverage is far leaner, stressing the immediate casualty count and the strike as “one of the deadliest” under a “fragile” ceasefire, while de-emphasizing broader rescue and combat context.
The main dividing line is whether the story is about civilian suffering and ceasefire erosion or about a discrete military event.
Both sides agree the truce is strained and violence persists, reflecting deeper regional and ideological tensions.
summary: Israeli airstrikes on southern Lebanon killed at least 19 people on Tuesday, including four women and three children, Lebanon's Health Ministry said, continuing violence despite the fragile, United States-brokered ceasefire in the Israel-Hezbollah war.

Combat between Israel and Hezbollah has intensified despite a 45-day extension of the United States-brokered truce begun on April 17, with the death toll in this round of fighting recently surpassing 3,000.

Strikes in Deir Qanoun, Nabatieh, and Kfar Sir killed 10, four, and five people respectively, while state-run National News Agency reported strikes destroyed houses, leaving civilians trapped under rubble.

Between Monday afternoon and Tuesday afternoon, the Israeli military targeted more than 25 sites of Hezbollah infrastructure in southern Lebanon, while one soldier died in battle, raising Israel's troop death toll to 21.
More than a million people have been displaced in Lebanon by the fighting, with many sheltering in tents along the Mediterranean Sea in Beirut, as persistent attacks strain the fragile ceasefire.
title: Israeli Strikes on Southern Lebanon Kill 19, Including Children and Women, Officials Say

bias: Left-Leaning coverage centers on the ambassador’s claim that the column is “fueling” a “dramatic rise in antisemitism” and stresses the internal backlash at the newspaper, portraying the episode as a media scandal with “acute tensions” and reputational fallout.

Right-Leaning coverage is far more combative, using phrases like “woke journalism,” “blood libel,” “miscarriage of journalism,” and “war against Israel” to frame the same column as ideological hostility and moral wrongdoing; it also elevates Israel’s threatened legal action and the paper’s defense as part of the clash.
Both sides agree the story triggered outrage, but they diverge on whether the core issue is newsroom controversy or antisemitic bias.
summary: New York Times columnist Nicholas Kristof sparked controversy with an op-ed titled "The Silence That Meets the Rape of Palestinians," claiming a pattern of widespread Israeli sexual violence based on interviews with 14 alleged victims.
Critics questioned the op-ed's credibility, citing inconsistencies in testimonies from sources Sami al-Sai and Issa Amro, who gave conflicting accounts to different outlets about alleged assaults.
Experts including dog trainer Reuven Weinstein disputed the piece's allegation that Israeli guards trained dogs to sexually assault detainees, calling the claim biologically impossible.

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Foreign Minister Gideon Saar announced Thursday they directed a defamation lawsuit against the New York Times, with Netanyahu calling the article a "blood libel."

The Foreign Ministry accused the publication of deliberately timing the piece to undermine a Civil Commission report on October 7 crimes by Hamas; Kristof acknowledged lacking evidence of state-ordered rape but argued sexual violence remains routine.
title: A Miscarriage of Journalism at ‘The New York Times’

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the episode as an Israeli military interception of “activists” and “aid” seekers, stressing detention and blockade-breaking, while right-leaning coverage more often spotlights the humanitarian mission, international scope, and the procedural step of detainees being taken toward Israel , with the left using sharper, more emotive cues like “intercepted” and “detained” and the right leaning on calmer terms like “reported” and “humanitarian aid.” A key dividing line is whether the flotilla is portrayed as a civic challenge to blockade or as a security-control event; center coverage sits between them by adding numbers—“12 Canadians,” “500 activists,” “50 boats”—without the stronger partisan coloring.
Both sides agree on the interception and detentions, but they invert the moral emphasis around blockade, aid, and state force.
summary: On Monday, the Israeli military intercepted the Global Sumud Flotilla off the coast of Cyprus, detaining six Canadians among activists attempting to break the Gaza blockade.
Israel has maintained a blockade of Gaza for 18 years to prevent Hamas from smuggling weapons, though critics characterize the restriction as collective punishment.
Boarding the vessels 250 nautical miles from Gaza, Israeli forces intercepted the boats in daylight; activist Shahid Sadabad reported the Canadian contingent planned a hunger strike if detained.
The Israeli Foreign Ministry labeled the mission a "provocation for the sake of provocation," while Global Affairs Canada acknowledged the request for comment but has not yet provided a response.
Forces previously detained 175 activists near Crete during an April 30 operation, demonstrating the ongoing pattern of maritime interceptions blocking humanitarian aid missions to Gaza.
title: Six Canadians detained by Israel off coast of Cyprus: flotilla organizer

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the poll as a mixed verdict—Trump “falters” on the economy but shows “resilience” on immigration and overall approval—while right-leaning coverage sharpens the story into a broader slide, stressing that approval “slips,” support “weakens,” and cracks are opening in his base.

The left’s tone is more comparative and guarded, using words like “falters” and “unhappier” to spotlight specific weakness without collapsing into total decline; the right uses more alarming, cumulative language to imply wider erosion.
The key dividing lines are the economy versus overall standing, and whether continued backing amid Iran outweighs rising Republican dissatisfaction.
summary: A new AP-NORC poll finds Republicans are less satisfied with President Donald Trump 's handling of the economy than earlier this year, though they largely continue supporting him amid the war with Iran.
While a majority of Republicans, 63%, still approve of Trump's economic management, that figure is down from 79% earlier this year, before the war with Iran began fueling higher gasoline prices.

Ariel Gutierrez, a 55-year-old Republican in Wisconsin, noted spiking gas costs are impacting family leisure, while Richard Baumgartner, a 77-year-old Republican from Las Vegas, called higher costs a "necessary side effect" of the war.
Despite economic skepticism, Trump's political hold remains strong, evidenced Tuesday when his handpicked candidate defeated Rep Thomas Massie, a Trump critic, in a primary challenge.
Immigration remains one of Trump's stronger issues, with 45% of American adults approving, while his overall presidential approval rating stands at 37%, up slightly from 33% in April.
title: Where Trump is Losing and Keeping Republican Support: AP-NORC Poll

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the case as an “unconstitutional retaliation campaign,” stressing the March lawsuit, “deliberately indifferent” allegations, and political overreach; right-leaning coverage shifts toward Harvard ’s defense, highlighting that the DOJ “failed to prove” or state a claim, while also spotlighting Harvard’s “sustained, institution-wide efforts” against antisemitism.

The sharpest split is rhetorical: left uses “retaliatory” and constitutional language, while right leans on more charged phrasing like “Jew-hatred” and broader conflict terms around funding conditions, diversity, and campus governance.
Both sides agree the dispute is a federal court fight over antisemitism, but they diverge on whether it is chiefly about punishment or accountability.
summary: On Monday, Harvard asked a federal judge to dismiss the DOJ's antisemitism lawsuit, requesting the case be transferred to District Judge Allison D. Burroughs.
The Trump administration threatened to withhold $9 billion in funding last April unless Harvard University overhauled its leadership, admissions, and hiring practices.

Harvard lawyers argued The Government failed to conduct a 'meaningful investigation' of campus antisemitism, contending, 'The Government does not even attempt to allege an ongoing violation of Title VI.'

District Judge Allison D. Burroughs previously reversed billions in federal funding cuts in September, calling the administration's antisemitism rationale a 'smokescreen' before The Government appealed in December.

This litigation follows The Trump administration's broader campaign targeting diversity programs across federally funded institutions, as Harvard maintains it condemns antisemitism while asserting the suit lacks legal standing.
title: Harvard asks judge to dismiss Trump antisemitism suit
